pattern: Comparing squared diagonal lengths avoids floating point entirely, and a simple tie-break on area during a single scan finds the answer in one pass.
hints: You never need the actual diagonal length, only which rectangle's diagonal is longest, so compare length^2 + width^2 directly.
Scan the rectangles once, keeping the best squared-diagonal seen so far and the area that goes with it.
When a new rectangle ties the best squared diagonal exactly, keep whichever of the two has the larger area.

edge_inputs.description: one rectangle has the strictly longer diagonal
two rectangles tie on diagonal length, larger area wins
a square has the same diagonal as a non-square with less area
three-way tie on diagonal, area must still pick correctly

stress.description: many rectangles with random sides across the full range
stress.note: covers the full 100-rectangle boundary with random dimensions

oracle_python:
class Solution:
    def areaOfMaxDiagonal(self, dimensions):
        # Independent implementation using an actual floating-point diagonal
        # comparison instead of the squared-length trick.
        import math
        best_len = -1.0
        best_area = 0
        for l, w in dimensions:
            diag = math.sqrt(l * l + w * w)
            area = l * w
            if diag > best_len + 1e-9 or (abs(diag - best_len) <= 1e-9 and area > best_area):
                best_len = diag
                best_area = area
        return best_area
